title: What is the CIA Triad?
content: The CIA Triad is the foundational model for information security. It stands for Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability. Every security control, policy, and practice maps back to protecting one or more of these three properties.

title: Confidentiality
content: Ensuring that information is accessible only to those authorized to have access. Controls include encryption, access controls, and data classification.

title: Integrity
content: Ensuring that information and systems are not modified by unauthorized parties. Controls include hashing, digital signatures, and change management.

title: Availability
content: Ensuring that information and systems are accessible when needed by authorized users. Controls include redundancy, backups, and disaster recovery.

title: CIA Triad on the Exam
content: When an exam question describes a scenario, identify which CIA property is being threatened. Data leaked = Confidentiality. Data altered = Integrity. Service down = Availability.

title: CIA Triad Summary
content: The CIA Triad is the lens through which all security decisions are evaluated. Most exam questions can be mapped to one of these three principles.

title: Authentication Fundamentals
content: Authentication is the process of verifying the identity of a user, device, or system. It answers the question: Are you who you claim to be? Authentication is distinct from authorization (what you can do) and accounting (what you did).

title: Something You Know
content: Knowledge-based factors such as passwords, PINs, and security questions. These are the most common but weakest form of authentication because they can be guessed, stolen, or shared.

title: Something You Have
content: Possession-based factors such as smart cards, hardware tokens, or a mobile phone receiving an SMS code. Stronger than knowledge factors because physical possession is required.

title: Something You Are
content: Biometric factors such as fingerprints, facial recognition, iris scans, or voice patterns. These are the hardest to forge but raise privacy concerns and cannot be changed if compromised.

title: MFA on the Exam
content: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) requires two or more different factor types. Using a password + PIN is NOT MFA because both are 'something you know.' Password + fingerprint IS MFA.

title: Authentication Summary
content: Strong authentication uses multiple factor categories. The three categories are: something you know, something you have, and something you are.

title: Access Control Overview
content: Access control determines who can access what resources and under what conditions. It is enforced after authentication and is a critical component of the security architecture.

title: DAC (Discretionary Access Control)
content: The resource owner decides who gets access. Common in Windows/Linux file systems. Flexible but risky because users can share access without oversight.

title: MAC (Mandatory Access Control)
content: Access is determined by security labels and clearance levels set by a central authority. Users cannot change permissions. Used in military and government systems.

title: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)
content: Access is assigned based on organizational roles rather than individual identity. An employee in the 'HR' role gets HR permissions. Most common in enterprises.

title: Access Control Models on the Exam
content: If the question mentions labels, classifications, or clearances, it is MAC. If it mentions the owner setting permissions, it is DAC. If it mentions job roles or groups, it is RBAC.

title: What is a Firewall?
content: A firewall is a network security device that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing traffic based on predetermined security rules. It establishes a barrier between trusted internal networks and untrusted external networks.

title: Packet Filtering Firewall
content: Examines each packet header (source/destination IP, port, protocol) and applies rules. Fast but cannot inspect payload content or track connection state.

title: Stateful Inspection Firewall
content: Tracks the state of active connections and makes decisions based on context of the traffic, not just individual packets. More secure than packet filtering.

title: Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)
content: Combines traditional firewall capabilities with deep packet inspection, intrusion prevention, application awareness, and threat intelligence feeds.

title: Firewall Types on the Exam
content: Know the progression: packet filtering (Layer 3-4) < stateful inspection (tracks sessions) < NGFW (Layer 7, app-aware). Questions often test which type can inspect application-layer data.

title: VPN Fundamentals
content: A Virtual Private Network (VPN) creates an encrypted tunnel over a public network, allowing secure remote access to private resources. VPNs protect data in transit using encryption protocols.

title: IPSec
content: Internet Protocol Security operates at the network layer (Layer 3). Uses two modes: Transport (encrypts payload only) and Tunnel (encrypts entire packet). Common for site-to-site VPNs.

title: SSL/TLS VPN
content: Operates at the session layer using HTTPS. Easier to set up than IPSec, works through web browsers, and is common for remote access VPNs.

title: VPN Protocols on the Exam
content: IPSec = site-to-site, Layer 3. SSL/TLS VPN = remote access, Layer 5+. If the question says 'browser-based' or 'clientless', it is SSL/TLS VPN.

title: IDS and IPS Overview
content: Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) monitor network traffic for suspicious activity and alert administrators. Intrusion Prevention Systems (IPS) can also automatically block detected threats.

title: Signature-Based Detection
content: Compares traffic patterns against a database of known attack signatures. Very accurate for known threats but cannot detect zero-day or novel attacks.

title: Anomaly-Based Detection
content: Establishes a baseline of normal network behavior and flags deviations. Can detect unknown threats but has a higher false positive rate.

title: IDS vs IPS on the Exam
content: IDS = passive, detect and alert only. IPS = active, detect and block. If the question asks what can prevent an attack in real-time, the answer is IPS.

title: Detection Summary
content: Use signature-based for known threats with low false positives. Use anomaly-based for detecting unknown threats at the cost of more false positives. Defense in depth uses both.

title: Symmetric Encryption Overview
content: Symmetric encryption uses the same key for both encryption and decryption. It is fast and efficient for large amounts of data but has the key distribution problem -- both parties must securely share the secret key.

title: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)
content: The current standard for symmetric encryption. Uses block cipher with key sizes of 128, 192, or 256 bits. AES-256 is considered secure against brute force attacks.

title: Block vs Stream Ciphers
content: Block ciphers encrypt fixed-size blocks of data (e.g., AES encrypts 128-bit blocks). Stream ciphers encrypt data one bit or byte at a time (e.g., RC4). Block ciphers are more common in modern systems.

title: Symmetric Key on the Exam
content: If the question mentions a shared secret key or fast bulk encryption, think symmetric. AES is the go-to answer for symmetric encryption questions.

title: Asymmetric Encryption Overview
content: Asymmetric encryption uses a key pair: a public key (shared freely) and a private key (kept secret). Data encrypted with one key can only be decrypted with the other. Solves the key distribution problem but is much slower than symmetric encryption.

title: RSA
content: The most widely used asymmetric algorithm. Based on the difficulty of factoring large prime numbers. Common key sizes are 2048 and 4096 bits. Used for key exchange, digital signatures, and encryption.

title: Key Exchange
content: The process of securely sharing encryption keys. Diffie-Hellman allows two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel without transmitting the key itself.

title: Asymmetric on the Exam
content: Public key encrypts, private key decrypts. Private key signs, public key verifies. If the question involves digital signatures, non-repudiation, or key exchange, think asymmetric.

title: Hashing Overview
content: A hash function takes input data of any size and produces a fixed-length output (digest). Hashing is one-way -- you cannot reverse a hash to get the original data. Used for integrity verification, password storage, and digital signatures.

title: SHA (Secure Hash Algorithm)
content: SHA-256 produces a 256-bit hash. Part of the SHA-2 family. SHA-1 (160-bit) is deprecated due to collision vulnerabilities. SHA-256 is the current standard.

title: MD5
content: Produces a 128-bit hash. Considered broken for security purposes due to known collision attacks. Still used for non-security checksums (file downloads).

title: Salting and Stretching
content: A salt is random data added to input before hashing to prevent rainbow table attacks. Stretching (e.g., bcrypt, PBKDF2) applies the hash function many times to slow down brute force attacks.

title: Hashing on the Exam
content: MD5 = broken, don't use for security. SHA-256 = current standard. If the question asks about password storage, the answer involves salting + hashing, never encryption.

title: Hashing Summary
content: Hashing provides integrity, not confidentiality. Always salt passwords before hashing. Use SHA-256 or better. MD5 and SHA-1 are deprecated for security use.
